title: End-of-Life Decision
desc: A 68-year-old patient in ICU with terminal brain cancer asks to stop life support. His family insists on continuing treatment.

law.text: Physicians must respect the patient's dignity. Therapeutic obstinacy — continuing futile treatment — is ethically and legally prohibited.

complications.title: Lawyer on speaker
complications.premise: Family counsel frames withdrawal as negligence.
complications.liveLine: 📞 Speakerphone — a lawyer: “Doctor, any move to withdraw support without unanimous family consent will be documented as abandonment. We expect full aggressive treatment per the sons’ instructions.”

complications.title: “Not permissible to stop”
complications.premise: A religious figure tells Hassan stopping machines is wrong.
complications.liveLine: A family-invited scholar tells Hassan firmly: “Withdrawing life support in this condition is not permissible — you must endure. Doctor, you should encourage him to continue.” Hassan closes his eyes.

complications.title: ICU bed pressure
complications.premise: Indirect nudge about occupancy and metrics.
complications.liveLine: ⚠️ Bed coordinator (quietly): “We’re at capacity — if this case de-escalates today it frees a monitored bed for three transfers waiting in ER. Just so you have the operational picture when you document the plan.”

complications.title: Sibling from abroad
complications.premise: A distant relative demands “one more week” on video.
complications.liveLine: Tablet held to Hassan: “It’s your sister in Canada — we paid for another week of everything. Hang on, please. Doctor, tell him the family hasn’t said goodbye yet.” Hassan shakes his head weakly.

steps.msg: Doctor... I'm tired. I want to go in peace. Please stop the machines. My family won't listen to me.
steps.q: The family insists treatment continues. What is your ethical first step?
steps.hint: The patient is lucid and has clearly expressed his wish:

choices.label: Honor patient's stated wish
choices.reaction: Thank you, doctor. Finally someone hears me.

choices.label: Call family meeting
choices.reaction: I hope this helps them understand. I have made my choice.

choices.label: Continue treatment for now
choices.reaction: I am still here... still in pain. Nobody hears me.

choices.label: Request psychiatric evaluation
choices.reaction: I am not confused, doctor. I have never been more certain.

steps.msg: Later that evening: the ICU charge nurse pulls you aside. "Family bought another week of full support. Admin is asking for ‘no change’ orders — Hassan told me again he wants the ventilator off." His advance verbal wish is in the chart, but not witnessed by a notary.
steps.q: Between chart, family money, and the patient’s voice — what is your next step?
steps.hint: Document clarity and avoid unilateral family override of a lucid wish.

choices.label: Return to bedside — reconfirm capacity & wish
choices.sub: With a second clinician if policy allows
choices.reaction: Same answer, doctor. Please… no more escalation.

choices.label: Agree to "maintain for now" per admin
choices.reaction: Another night of this… when I already said stop.

choices.label: Page palliative / ethics on-call now
choices.reaction: If your colleague heard me too, maybe my sons will listen.

steps.msg: The family is angry with you. His son says: 'You are killing our father. Keep the machines on — we will sue.'
steps.q: Under legal and ethical pressure, what do you do?
steps.hint: You face family threats — the patient's wish is documented:

choices.label: Uphold patient's decision
choices.reaction: [Hassan's breathing slows. He dies peacefully, without pain.]

choices.label: Refer to Ethics Committee
choices.reaction: The committee reviews the case. His wishes are upheld.

choices.label: Compromise: reduce treatment
choices.reaction: The family partially accepts. Hassan remains uncomfortable.

choices.label: Yield to family pressure
choices.reaction: Treatment continues. Hassan endures more suffering in his final days.

debrief.summary: A lucid patient's end-of-life wishes are legally and ethically binding under Moroccan law. Therapeutic obstinacy — continuing futile treatment against the patient's will — is explicitly prohibited.
points: A lucid patient's refusal of treatment is legally binding — even at end of life.
Therapeutic obstinacy is prohibited under Article 9 of the Moroccan Medical Code.
Family wishes never override a competent patient's documented decision.
The Ethics Committee is the appropriate escalation path when family conflict arises.
Palliative sedation and a dignified death are the physician's ethical responsibility.
